summary: This notebook demonstrates how to achieve a competitive leaderboard score (~0.684) on the WSDM Cup Multilingual Chatbot Arena using only the provided test data and a quantized Gemma-2 9B model. It walks through a complete inference pipeline that handles multilingual text preprocessing, custom prompt formatting, and dynamic batching across two GPUs. The author highlights how Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) via response swapping stabilizes predictions and shows how to efficiently run inference with PEFT/LoRA adapters without additional fine-tuning. Readers should learn how to adapt large language models for pairwise preference tasks using minimal resources and standard Hugging Face/PEFT tooling.
competition_flow: Reads the test parquet file, preprocesses and tokenizes prompts and responses with custom formatting, runs inference using a 4-bit quantized Gemma-2 9B model with LoRA adapters across two GPUs, applies TTA by averaging predictions from swapped responses, and outputs a CSV submission file.
data_processing: Fills NaN values with empty strings, truncates long texts using token offsets while inserting a '(snip)' marker, formats inputs with custom tags (<prompt>, <response_a>, <response_b>), tokenizes with GemmaTokenizerFast using dynamic padding and a max length of 1900, and implements TTA by swapping response_a and response_b.
ensembling: Averages the prediction probabilities from the original and swapped response pairs (TTA) to produce a final score, then applies a 0.5 threshold to assign 'model_a' or 'model_b'.
insights: Swapping response positions during inference acts as effective TTA and improves prediction stability without retraining.
Sorting inputs by length and using dynamic padding with multiple batch sizes maximizes GPU memory utilization and speeds up inference.
Loading the base model on two separate GPUs and processing batches in parallel with ThreadPoolExecutor significantly reduces total inference time.
